title: Mathematical Models in Biology
subtitle: Classics in Applied Mathematics

authors.name: Leah Edelstein-Keshet
knownFor: Professor of Mathematics at University of British Columbia
Pioneer in mathematical biology and cell motility modeling
Author of the field-defining textbook Mathematical Models in Biology
SIAM Fellow and former president of the Society for Mathematical Biology

description: Mathematical Models in Biology is the classic textbook that defined the field of mathematical biology for generations of students. First published in 1988 and reissued in 2005 by SIAM as part of its Classics in Applied Mathematics series, the book provides a comprehensive, rigorous introduction to the mathematics of biological systems. Edelstein-Keshet covers the full spectrum of modeling approaches: continuous and discrete population models, reaction kinetics, diffusion and pattern formation, along with the mathematical tools required to analyze them. The book is organized around biological problems — population growth, predator-prey dynamics, epidemics, enzyme kinetics, cellular dynamics, and morphogenesis — and develops the mathematics needed to address each one. Key mathematical techniques include phase plane analysis, linearization and stability, bifurcation theory, perturbation methods, and numerical simulation. The approach is consistently applied: each chapter begins with a biological question, develops the mathematical model, analyzes the model's behavior, and returns to the biological interpretation. The 2005 edition includes new material on stochastic models, spatial pattern formation, and the analysis of biological oscillations. It remains the standard graduate-level introduction to the field and is used in mathematics, biology, and interdisciplinary programs worldwide.
keyIdeas: Mathematical models are not reality — they are tools for understanding that must be validated against biological data and revised when they fail
The logistic differential equation dN/dt = rN(1-N/K) captures the fundamental trade-off between growth and carrying capacity in biological populations
Lotka-Volterra equations for predator-prey interactions produce neutrally stable cycles that are structurally unstable — a limitation that drove the development of more realistic models
Phase plane analysis is the primary tool for understanding two-variable systems: nullclines, equilibria, stability, and bifurcations reveal the qualitative behavior of biological systems
Reaction-diffusion systems can produce spatial patterns from homogeneous initial conditions through Turing instability — a mechanism for biological pattern formation
Compartmental models of epidemics (SIR: Susceptible-Infected-Recovered) are fundamental to understanding disease spread and control strategies
Enzyme kinetics follow Michaelis-Menten dynamics, which can be derived from mass-action kinetics using the quasi-steady-state approximation
Bifurcation theory explains how gradual parameter changes can produce sudden qualitative changes in biological system behavior — a key to understanding development and disease
Stochastic processes are essential when populations are small (molecular reactions, rare events) or when randomness fundamentally shapes outcomes
keyTakeaways: Always start with the simplest possible model that captures the essential biology; add complexity only when the simple model fails
Dimensional analysis reduces parameter counts and reveals the fundamental scaling relationships in any biological system
Stability analysis of equilibria — linearizing around fixed points — is the most widely applicable mathematical tool in theoretical biology
Bifurcation diagrams map how system behavior changes with parameters; they are the theoretical biologist's most important visual tool
Model validation requires comparison with data, sensitivity analysis, and experimental test — a model that fits data perfectly is not necessarily correct
Spatial models often produce counterintuitive results: diffusion can destabilize a system (Turing instability) or stabilize it, depending on the reaction kinetics
whoShouldRead: Graduate students and researchers in mathematical biology, bio-mathematics, and theoretical ecology
Biologists with strong quantitative backgrounds who want to incorporate modeling into their research
Applied mathematicians and physicists seeking biologically motivated problems and applications
Advanced undergraduates in mathematics or biology taking interdisciplinary modeling courses
whoShouldSkip: Readers without a solid foundation in calculus and ordinary differential equations
Those seeking a conceptual or qualitative overview of mathematical biology without mathematical detail
General readers looking for popular science or narrative nonfiction about biology
